agent1_snippets.title: Brief Psychotic Disorder -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Brief psychotic disorder (BPD) according to DSM-5 is the sudden onset of psychotic behavior that lasts less than 1 month followed by complete remission with possible future relapses. [1] It is differentiated from schizophreniform disorder and schizophrenia by the duration of the psychosis. The diagnosis is often anticipatory or retrospective due to the diagnostic requirement of complete remission within 1 month. Brief psychotic disorder is an acute but transient disorder with the onset of one or more of the following psychotic symptoms: Delusions Hallucinations Disorganized speech Grossly disorganized or catatonic behavior

agent1_snippets.title: Psychosis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
agent1_snippets.content: Specific subgroups of patients experiencing mental illness have been historically difficult to engage: those with first-episode psychosis, homeless populations, and those with comorbid substance use. Poor treatment engagement correlates with poor clinical outcomes, relapse, and re-hospitalization. A recent emphasis on “interpersonal” care has emerged in mental health treatment, putting the context of the individual’s needs, wants, hopes, dreams, culture, and spirituality above those of their specific symptoms. The promulgation of this concept centers on training of “treating the whole patient,” and it firmly holds for patients with mental illness. This activity will focus below on three emerging, innovative, recovery-oriented techniques for engagement. [27]

agent1_snippets.title: Brief Psychotic Disorder -- Prognosis
agent1_snippets.content: Given the nature of this condition, the prognosis is considerably well with complete remission of symptoms within a month per definition based on DSM-5 criteria. However, the symptoms may recur especially in the setting of a stressful psychosocial milieu. Some positive prognostic indicators for the brief psychotic disorder are the absence of genetically related individuals with schizophrenia or brief psychotic disorder, sudden symptom onset, the presence of stressful triggers, and a short duration of symptoms.

agent1_snippets.title: Psychosis -- Differential Diagnosis
agent1_snippets.content: Age of onset: This is one of the most important factors when determining the etiology of a psychotic episode. The primary psychotic disorder will usually present in the late teens to the early thirties. (Men typically present with the condition earlier than women). Psychosis associated with medical/neurological conditions will often present after the age of 40. The older the patient, the higher the risk for medical or neurological psychosis, especially in the hospital setting.

agent1_snippets.title: Violent Patients -- Prognosis
agent1_snippets.content: According to the 2023 ACEP guidelines, 1st-line medications for undifferentiated violent patients include a combination of intramuscularly injected haloperidol 5 mg, lorazepam 2 mg, ziprasidone 20 mg, and olanzapine 10 mg. However, ziprasidone and olanzapine must be administered with caution.

agent1_snippets.title: Shared Psychotic Disorder -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: This disorder was first listed in the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Third Edition ( DSM-III) as shared paranoid disorder. In the next edition (DSM-IV), the term changed to shared psychotic disorder. In the latest edition, DSM-5, it was removed as a separate disease entity. It now is included in the section on other specified schizophrenia spectrum and other psychotic disorders. ICD-10 lists it as induced delusional disorder. [2]

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 677
agent2_snippets.content: Transient organic psychotic conditions (حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة) are recognized as a subtype of organic psychosis (ذُهان عضوي) and are classified within both anxiety disorders and mood disorders. The diagnostic category “Other specified transient mental disorders due to conditions classified elsewhere” is linked as a child of both mood disorders and anxiety disorders, indicating that it shares features of each. The depressive type of transient organic psychotic condition (Transient organic psychotic condition, depressive type) is closely related to mood disorders and may be considered synonymous with mood‑disorder presentations. Together, these relationships illustrate that certain transient mental disorders span the domains of anxiety, mood, and organic psychosis, highlighting the importance of an integrated diagnostic approach for accurate classification and optimal patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 435
agent2_snippets.content: The classification “الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ)” is presented as a broad grouping that encompasses a variety of psychotic and affective conditions. Within this group are several specific entities: “ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة” (unspecified childhood psychosis, current or active), “هوس شَبَقِيّ” (schizotypal mania), and “عاطفة متقلصة” (blunted affect). In addition to primary psychotic disorders, the SMQ grouping also includes mood‑related diagnoses such as “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة راجعة” (major depressive disorder, recurrent episode), “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة” (major depressive disorder, single episode), “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة في هدأة تامة” (major depressive disorder, single episode in full remission), “اكتئاب أَوبِيّ” (atypical depression), and “التقلقل الوجداني” (affective lability). This reflects an integrated view in which depressive disorders and affective symptoms are considered part of the broader psychosis spectrum, especially when they may present with psychotic features. The entity “ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة” is also linked to the more general category “disorders usually diagnosed in infancy,” indicating that this form of childhood psychosis is typically identified early in life. Furthermore, “هوس شَبَقِيّ” is related to “ذُهان عضوي” (organic psychosis) through a broader‑relationship link, suggesting that schizotypal mania can be viewed as a subtype or manifestation of an organic psychotic process. Overall, the SMQ framework integrates early‑onset psychotic conditions, organic psychoses, specific manic presentations, and a range of depressive and affective disorders, highlighting the clinical overlap among psychotic and mood disturbances and underscoring the importance of considering these interrelationships in diagnosis and patient management.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 956
agent2_snippets.content: Mental symptom is a broad clinical concept that includes diverse manifestations such as personality problems, loss of pleasure (anhedonia), and self‑destructive behavior. These mental symptoms are linked to psychotic conditions; specifically, they have a broader relationship with transient organic psychotic states (حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة). Transient organic psychotic states are a subtype of organic psychosis (ذُهان عضوي), which itself is broadly related to schizophrenic delusion (هوس شَبَقِيّ). The overarching category of psychosis (الذهان) therefore encompasses transient organic psychotic states, situating them within the spectrum of organic psychosis and its association with schizophrenic delusional phenomena. This integrated view highlights how specific mental symptoms connect to distinct forms of organic psychosis and their broader psychotic correlates.
